mnemonics.title: String Case Methods
mnemonics.content: TLOWER: toLowerCase() returns NEW string, never modifies original. Tupper: toUpperCase() also returns NEW string.

mnemonics.title: Turkish Locale Issue
mnemonics.content: T.I.DOT: Turkish İ (dotted I) - 'i'→'İ' is special, use Locale.TURKISH for proper conversion to avoid wrong 'I'.

mnemonics.title: Immutability Benefit
mnemonics.content: S.E.C: Security, Efficiency (hashing), Concurrency - the three reasons for String immutability.

acronyms.expansion: Locale Object for Conversion - use when converting strings for display to users in specific regions
International Components for Unicode - Java uses ICU for locale-sensitive operations
acronyms.usage: Understanding ICU helps debug locale issues in case conversion

visualTips.visualization: Picture a String as carved in stone - you can't change it, you must create a new carved stone (new String) for any modifications
Think of toLowerCase().toUpperCase() like passing a document through two photocopiers - each step creates a new copy, the original stays unchanged
Draw a table: English: i→I, Turkish: i→İ (with dot!). The dotted İ has a tiny tent (¨) on top, remember the dot matters!

analogies.analogy: Like a printed newspaper - once printed, you can't change it. You can only create a new edition (new String) with corrections.
Like putting on sunglasses - you don't change your eyes (original string), you add a filter that changes how things look (new string).
Like translating for a British vs American audience - 'colour' vs 'color'. The Turkish 'İ' is a different 'letter' that Americans don't have, causing confusion if you use the wrong conversion rules.
